risk.desc: Potential thermal management issues due to battery pack proximity to controller board.
risk.mit: Implement additional thermal barriers or cooling solutions between battery and controller.

risk.desc: Risk of electromagnetic interference (EMI) between power and signal lines if proper separation is not maintained.
risk.mit: Ensure power and signal lines are routed separately or shielded appropriately.

risk.desc: Serviceability concerns if cable exit ports are not properly aligned with adjacent modules.
risk.mit: Verify cable exit port alignment during assembly and ensure clear labeling.

risk.desc: Risk of structural integrity compromise if snap-fit latches are not designed with sufficient strength.
risk.mit: Conduct stress analysis on snap-fit latches and reinforce if necessary.

risk.desc: Potential grounding issues if return paths are not clearly defined and implemented.
risk.mit: Document grounding schemes and ensure signal returns are routed alongside signals.

generated_parts.label: Wiring Loom + Service Loop Set
generated_parts.spec: Knuckle-crossing wiring loom with service loops, strain relief at every flex boundary, flex-rated bend radius, power vs signal channel separation

requirements: Knuckle-crossing wiring with service loops and flex-rated bend radius
Service loop, strain relief, bend radius note
Battery, sensor, actuator, cartridge, payload, cable, or joint modules remain removable without full teardown
Harnesses, tubes, or service channels are routed through visible raceways or protected channels
Access is represented by geometry: hatch, pocket, channel, fastener pattern, slot, window, or removable cover
Every cable exit shows strain relief geometry (clamp, grommet, tie point) and routing respects a stated minimum bend radius away from moving joints and sharp edges
Connectors are reachable without disassembling structure, have finger/tool clearance, and mention keying or labeling to prevent mis-mating
Per-finger linkage cartridges are removable by single pin or visible hinge access
Flexion-resistance actuator modules plug in and remain visible near knuckle row
Rigid mock assembly preserves soft-contact cues, wrist quick release, and ergonomic clearance
Finger and wrist wiring uses service loops, strain relief at every flex boundary, and a stated bend radius sized for repeated flexion cycles
Motor power lines and sensor/encoder signal lines run in separate channels or are spaced/crossed at right angles, with the separation visible in routing geometry
Each electronics module states its grounding scheme (star point, chassis bond, isolated) and signal returns route alongside their signals
PCBs mount on standoffs with keep-out clearance to walls and moving parts, connector edges face access openings, and mounting holes match standard board patterns

Every inter-part interface names a specific connection type (bolted, threaded_insert, snap_fit, press_fit, dovetail_slide, clevis_pin, revolute_bearing, band_clamp, adhesive_lap, o_ring, connector_mate, harness_passthrough) with its DOF and a numeric clearance

Parts can be installed in a stated order where every fastener, connector, and mating feature stays reachable; no installed part blocks access to a later one; last-installed and captive parts are identified
One primary datum part anchors the assembly; each other part locates off a named parent datum using a 3-2-1 scheme, so tolerance does not accumulate uncontrolled across the stack
Every part's primary load traces through named interfaces to the base/ground; the load path closes for all parts and high-load joints use load-appropriate connection types
The assembly uses the fewest interfaces that still allow service and manufacture; each remaining interface is justified by service, motion, manufacture split, or load transfer
